1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: require "active_support/core_ext/enumerable"
4: 
5: module ActiveRecord
6:   module Associations
7:     # = Active Record Association Collection
8:     #
9:     # CollectionAssociation is an abstract class that provides common stuff to
10:     # ease the implementation of association proxies that represent
11:     # collections. See the class hierarchy in Association.
12:     #
13:     #   CollectionAssociation:
14:     #     HasManyAssociation => has_many
15:     #       HasManyThroughAssociation + ThroughAssociation => has_many :through
16:     #
17:     # The CollectionAssociation class provides common methods to the collections
18:     # defined by +has_and_belongs_to_many+, +has_many+ or +has_many+ with
19:     # the <tt>:through association</tt> option.
20:     #
21:     # You need to be careful with assumptions regarding the target: The proxy
22:     # does not fetch records from the database until it needs them, but new
23:     # ones created with +build+ are added to the target. So, the target may be
24:     # non-empty and still lack children waiting to be read from the database.
25:     # If you look directly to the database you cannot assume that's the entire
26:     # collection because new records may have been added to the target, etc.
27:     #
28:     # If you need to work on all current children, new and existing records,
29:     # +load_target+ and the +loaded+ flag are your friends.
30:     class CollectionAssociation < Association # :nodoc:
31:       attr_accessor :nested_attributes_target
32: 
33:       # Implements the reader method, e.g. foo.items for Foo.has_many :items
34:       def reader
35:         ensure_klass_exists!
36: 
37:         if stale_target?
38:           reload
39:         end
40: 
41:         @proxy ||= CollectionProxy.create(klass, self)
42:         @proxy.reset_scope
43:       end
44: 
45:       # Implements the writer method, e.g. foo.items= for Foo.has_many :items
46:       def writer(records)
47:         replace(records)
48:       end
49: 
50:       # Implements the ids reader method, e.g. foo.item_ids for Foo.has_many :items
51:       def ids_reader
52:         if loaded?
53:           target.pluck(*reflection.association_primary_key)
54:         elsif !target.empty?
55:           load_target.pluck(*reflection.association_primary_key)
56:         else
57:           @association_ids ||= scope.pluck(*reflection.association_primary_key)
58:         end
59:       end
60: 
61:       # Implements the ids writer method, e.g. foo.item_ids= for Foo.has_many :items
62:       def ids_writer(ids)
63:         primary_key = reflection.association_primary_key
64:         ids = Array(ids).compact_blank
65: 
66:         records = if klass.composite_primary_key?
67:           pk_types = primary_key.map { |column| klass.type_for_attribute(column) }
68:           ids.map! { |id| pk_types.zip(id).map! { |type, value| type.cast(value) } }
69: 
70:           klass.where(primary_key => ids).index_by do |record|
71:             primary_key.map { |primary_key| record._read_attribute(primary_key) }
72:           end
73:         else
74:           pk_type = klass.type_for_attribute(primary_key)
75:           ids.map! { |id| pk_type.cast(id) }
76: 
77:           klass.where(primary_key => ids).index_by do |record|
78:             record._read_attribute(primary_key)
79:           end
80:         end.values_at(*ids).compact
81: 
82:         if records.size != ids.size
83:           found_ids = records.map { |record| record._read_attribute(primary_key) }
84:           not_found_ids = ids - found_ids
85:           klass.all.raise_record_not_found_exception!(ids, records.size, ids.size, primary_key, not_found_ids)
86:         else
87:           replace(records)
88:         end
89:       end
90: 
91:       def reset
92:         super
93:         @target = []
94:         @replaced_or_added_targets = Set.new.compare_by_identity
95:         @association_ids = nil
96:       end
97: 
98:       def find(*args)
99:         if options[:inverse_of] && loaded?
100:           args_flatten = args.flatten
101:           model = scope.model
102: 
103:           if args_flatten.blank?
104:             error_message = "Couldn't find #{model.name} without an ID"
105:             raise RecordNotFound.new(error_message, model.name, model.primary_key, args)
106:           end
107: 
108:           result = find_by_scan(*args)
109: 
110:           result_size = Array(result).size
111:           if !result || result_size != args_flatten.size
112:             scope.raise_record_not_found_exception!(args_flatten, result_size, args_flatten.size)
113:           else
114:             result
115:           end
116:         else
117:           scope.find(*args)
118:         end
119:       end
120: 
121:       def build(attributes = nil, &block)
122:         if attributes.is_a?(Array)
123:           attributes.collect { |attr| build(attr, &block) }
124:         else
125:           add_to_target(build_record(attributes, &block), replace: true)
126:         end
127:       end
128: 
129:       # Add +records+ to this association. Since +<<+ flattens its argument list
130:       # and inserts each record, +push+ and +concat+ behave identically.
131:       def concat(*records)
132:         records = records.flatten
133:         if owner.new_record?
134:           skip_strict_loading { load_target }
135:           concat_records(records)
136:         else
137:           transaction { concat_records(records) }
138:         end
139:       end
140: 
141:       # Removes all records from the association without calling callbacks
142:       # on the associated records. It honors the +:dependent+ option. However
143:       # if the +:dependent+ value is +:destroy+ then in that case the +:delete_all+
144:       # deletion strategy for the association is applied.
145:       #
146:       # You can force a particular deletion strategy by passing a parameter.
147:       #
148:       # Example:
149:       #
150:       # @author.books.delete_all(:nullify)
151:       # @author.books.delete_all(:delete_all)
152:       #
153:       # See delete for more info.
154:       def delete_all(dependent = nil)
155:         if dependent && ![:nullify, :delete_all].include?(dependent)
156:           raise ArgumentError, "Valid values are :nullify or :delete_all"
157:         end
158: 
159:         dependent = if dependent
160:           dependent
161:         elsif options[:dependent] == :destroy
162:           :delete_all
163:         else
164:           options[:dependent]
165:         end
166: 
167:         delete_or_nullify_all_records(dependent).tap do
168:           reset
169:           loaded!
170:         end
171:       end
172: 
173:       # Destroy all the records from this association.
174:       #
175:       # See destroy for more info.
176:       def destroy_all
177:         destroy(load_target).tap do
178:           reset
179:           loaded!
180:         end
181:       end
182: 
183:       # Removes +records+ from this association calling +before_remove+ and
184:       # +after_remove+ callbacks.
185:       #
186:       # This method is abstract in the sense that +delete_records+ has to be
187:       # provided by descendants. Note this method does not imply the records
188:       # are actually removed from the database, that depends precisely on
189:       # +delete_records+. They are in any case removed from the collection.
190:       def delete(*records)
191:         delete_or_destroy(records, options[:dependent])
192:       end
193: 
194:       # Deletes the +records+ and removes them from this association calling
195:       # +before_remove+, +after_remove+, +before_destroy+ and +after_destroy+ callbacks.
196:       #
197:       # Note that this method removes records from the database ignoring the
198:       # +:dependent+ option.
199:       def destroy(*records)
200:         delete_or_destroy(records, :destroy)

5:     class AssociationScope # :nodoc:

21:       def scope(association)

23:         reflection = association.reflection
24:         scope = klass.unscoped
25:         owner = association.owner
26:         chain = get_chain(reflection, association, scope.alias_tracker)
27: 
28:         scope.extending! reflection.extensions
29:         scope = add_constraints(scope, owner, chain)
30:         scope.limit!(1) unless reflection.collection?

34:       def self.get_bind_values(owner, chain)

37: 
38:         binds.push(*last_reflection.join_id_for(owner))
39:         if last_reflection.type
40:           binds << owner.class.polymorphic_name
41:         end
42: 
43:         chain.each_cons(2).each do |reflection, next_reflection|
44:           if reflection.type
45:             binds << next_reflection.klass.polymorphic_name
46:           end
47:         end

49:       end

54:         def join(table, constraint)

64:           primary_key_foreign_key_pairs.each do |join_key, foreign_key|
65:             value = transform_value(owner._read_attribute(foreign_key))
66:             scope = apply_scope(scope, table, join_key, value)

71:             scope = apply_scope(scope, table, reflection.type, polymorphic_type)
72:           end

330:         # We have some records loaded from the database (persisted) and some that are

334:         # So the task of this method is to merge them according to the following rules:
335:         #
336:         #   * The final array must not have duplicates
337:         #   * The order of the persisted array is to be preserved
338:         #   * Any changes made to attributes on objects in the memory array are to be preserved
339:         #   * Otherwise, attributes should have the value found in the database
340:         def merge_target_lists(persisted, memory)
341:           return persisted if memory.empty?

343:           persisted.map! do |record|
344:             if mem_record = memory.delete(record)

347:                 mem_record._write_attribute(name, record[name])
348:               end
349: 
350:               mem_record
351:             else
352:               record
353:             end
354:           end
355:

357:         end

185:       def disallow_raw_sql!(args, permit: adapter_class.column_name_matcher) # :nodoc:
186:         unexpected = nil
187:         args.each do |arg|
188:           next if arg.is_a?(Symbol) || Arel.arel_node?(arg) || permit.match?(arg.to_s.strip)
189:           (unexpected ||= []) << arg
190:         end
191:

194:             "Dangerous query method (method whose arguments are used as raw " \
195:             "SQL) called with non-attribute argument(s): " \
196:             "#{unexpected.map(&:inspect).join(", ")}." \
197:             "This method should not be called with user-provided values, such as request " \
198:             "parameters or model attributes. Known-safe values can be passed " \
199:             "by wrapping them in Arel.sql()."
200:           )

205:         def replace_bind_variables(connection, statement, values)
206:           raise_if_bind_arity_mismatch(statement, statement.count("?"), values.size)
207:           bound = values.dup
208:           statement.gsub(/\?/) do
209:             replace_bind_variable(connection, bound.shift)
210:           end
211:         end
212: 
213:         def replace_bind_variable(connection, value)
214:           if ActiveRecord::Relation === value
215:             value.to_sql
216:           else
217:             quote_bound_value(connection, value)
218:           end
219:         end
220: 
221:         def replace_named_bind_variables(connection, statement, bind_vars)
222:           statement.gsub(/([:\\]?):([a-zA-Z]\w*)/) do |match|
223:             if $1 == ":" # skip PostgreSQL casts
224:               match # return the whole match
225:             elsif $1 == "\\" # escaped literal colon
226:               match[1..-1] # return match with escaping backlash char removed
227:             elsif bind_vars.include?(match = $2.to_sym)
228:               replace_bind_variable(connection, bind_vars[match])
229:             else
230:               raise PreparedStatementInvalid, "missing value for :#{match} in #{statement}"
231:             end
232:           end
233:         end
234: 
235:         def quote_bound_value(connection, value)
236:           if value.respond_to?(:map) && !value.acts_like?(:string)
237:             values = value.map { |v| v.respond_to?(:id_for_database) ? v.id_for_database : v }
238:             if values.empty?
239:               connection.quote(connection.cast_bound_value(nil))
240:             else
241:               values.map! { |v| connection.quote(connection.cast_bound_value(v)) }.join(",")
242:             end
243:           else
244:             value = value.id_for_database if value.respond_to?(:id_for_database)
245:             connection.quote(connection.cast_bound_value(value))
246:           end
247:         end
248: 
249:         def raise_if_bind_arity_mismatch(statement, expected, provided)
250:           unless expected == provided
251:             raise PreparedStatementInvalid, "wrong number of bind variables (#{provided} for #{expected}) in: #{statement}"
252:           end
253:         end
254:     end
255:   end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: module ActiveRecord
4:   module Encryption
5:     # Automatically expand encrypted arguments to support querying both encrypted and unencrypted data
6:     #
7:     # Active Record \Encryption supports querying the db using deterministic attributes. For example:
8:     #
9:     #   Contact.find_by(email_address: "jorge@hey.com")
10:     #
11:     # The value "jorge@hey.com" will get encrypted automatically to perform the query. But there is
12:     # a problem while the data is being encrypted. This won't work. During that time, you need these
13:     # queries to be:
14:     #
15:     #   Contact.find_by(email_address: [ "jorge@hey.com", "<encrypted jorge@hey.com>" ])
16:     #
17:     # This patches ActiveRecord to support this automatically. It addresses both:
18:     #
19:     # * ActiveRecord::Base - Used in <tt>Contact.find_by_email_address(...)</tt>
20:     # * ActiveRecord::Relation - Used in <tt>Contact.internal.find_by_email_address(...)</tt>
21:     #
22:     # This module is included if `config.active_record.encryption.extend_queries` is `true`.
23:     module ExtendedDeterministicQueries
24:       def self.install_support
25:         # ActiveRecord::Base relies on ActiveRecord::Relation (ActiveRecord::QueryMethods) but it does
26:         # some prepared statements caching. That's why we need to intercept +ActiveRecord::Base+ as soon
27:         # as it's invoked (so that the proper prepared statement is cached).
28:         ActiveRecord::Relation.prepend(RelationQueries)
29:         ActiveRecord::Base.include(CoreQueries)
30:         ActiveRecord::Encryption::EncryptedAttributeType.prepend(ExtendedEncryptableType)
31:       end
32: 
33:       # When modifying this file run performance tests in
34:       # +activerecord/test/cases/encryption/performance/extended_deterministic_queries_performance_test.rb+
35:       # to make sure performance overhead is acceptable.
36:       #
37:       # @TODO We will extend this to support previous "encryption context" versions in future iterations
38:       # @TODO Experimental. Support for every kind of query is pending
39:       # @TODO It should not patch anything if not needed (no previous schemes or no support for previous encryption schemes)
40: 
41:       module EncryptedQuery # :nodoc:
42:         class << self
43:           def process_arguments(owner, args, check_for_additional_values)
44:             owner = owner.model if owner.is_a?(Relation)
45: 
46:             return args if owner.deterministic_encrypted_attributes&.empty?
47: 
48:             if args.is_a?(Array) && (options = args.first).is_a?(Hash)
49:               options = options.transform_keys do |key|
50:                 if key.is_a?(Array)
51:                   key.map(&:to_s)
52:                 else
53:                   key.to_s
54:                 end
55:               end
56:               args[0] = options
57: 
58:               owner.deterministic_encrypted_attributes&.each do |attribute_name|
59:                 attribute_name = attribute_name.to_s
60:                 type = owner.type_for_attribute(attribute_name)
61:                 if !type.previous_types.empty? && value = options[attribute_name]
62:                   options[attribute_name] = process_encrypted_query_argument(value, check_for_additional_values, type)
63:                 end
64:               end
65:             end
66: 
67:             args
68:           end
69: 
70:           private
71:             def process_encrypted_query_argument(value, check_for_additional_values, type)
72:               return value if check_for_additional_values && value.is_a?(Array) && value.last.is_a?(AdditionalValue)
73: 
74:               case value
75:               when String, Array
76:                 list = Array(value)
77:                 list + list.flat_map do |each_value|
78:                   if check_for_additional_values && each_value.is_a?(AdditionalValue)
79:                     each_value
80:                   else
81:                     additional_values_for(each_value, type)
82:                   end
83:                 end
84:               else
85:                 value
86:               end
87:             end
88: 
89:             def additional_values_for(value, type)
90:               type.previous_types.collect do |additional_type|
91:                 AdditionalValue.new(value, additional_type)
92:               end
93:             end
94:         end
95:       end
96: 
97:       module RelationQueries
98:         def where(*args)
99:           super(*EncryptedQuery.process_arguments(self, args, true))
100:         end
101: 
102:         def exists?(*args)
103:           super(*EncryptedQuery.process_arguments(self, args, true))
104:         end
105: 
106:         def scope_for_create
107:           return super unless model.deterministic_encrypted_attributes&.any?
108: 
109:           scope_attributes = super
110:           wheres = where_values_hash
111: 
112:           model.deterministic_encrypted_attributes.each do |attribute_name|
113:             attribute_name = attribute_name.to_s
114:             values = wheres[attribute_name]
115:             if values.is_a?(Array) && values[1..].all?(AdditionalValue)
116:               scope_attributes[attribute_name] = values.first
117:             end
118:           end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true

3: module ActiveRecord
4:   # = Active Record Signed Id
5:   module SignedId
6:     extend ActiveSupport::Concern
7: 
8:     included do
9:       class_attribute :_signed_id_verifier, instance_accessor: false, instance_predicate: false
10: 
11:       ##
12:       # :singleton-method:
13:       # Set the secret used for the signed id verifier instance when using Active Record outside of \Rails.
14:       # Within \Rails, this is automatically set using the \Rails application key generator.
15:       class_attribute :signed_id_verifier_secret, instance_writer: false
16:       module DeprecateSignedIdVerifierSecret
17:         def signed_id_verifier_secret=(secret)
18:           ActiveRecord.deprecator.warn(<<~MSG)
19:             ActiveRecord::Base.signed_id_verifier_secret is deprecated and will be removed in Rails 8.2.
20: 
21:             If the secret is model-specific, set Model.signed_id_verifier instead.
22: 
23:             Otherwise, configure Rails.application.message_verifiers (or ActiveRecord.message_verifiers) with the secret.
24:           MSG
25: 
26:           super
27:         end
28:       end
29:       singleton_class.prepend DeprecateSignedIdVerifierSecret
30:     end
31: 
32:     module RelationMethods # :nodoc:
33:       def find_signed(...)
34:         scoping { model.find_signed(...) }
35:       end
36: 
37:       def find_signed!(...)
38:         scoping { model.find_signed!(...) }
39:       end
40:     end
41: 
42:     module ClassMethods
43:       # Lets you find a record based on a signed id that's safe to put into the world without risk of tampering.
44:       # This is particularly useful for things like password reset or email verification, where you want
45:       # the bearer of the signed id to be able to interact with the underlying record, but usually only within
46:       # a certain time period.
47:       #
48:       # You set the time period that the signed id is valid for during generation, using the instance method
49:       # <tt>signed_id(expires_in: 15.minutes)</tt>. If the time has elapsed before a signed find is attempted,
50:       # the signed id will no longer be valid, and nil is returned.
51:       #
52:       # It's possible to further restrict the use of a signed id with a purpose. This helps when you have a
53:       # general base model, like a User, which might have signed ids for several things, like password reset
54:       # or email verification. The purpose that was set during generation must match the purpose set when
55:       # finding. If there's a mismatch, nil is again returned.
56:       #
57:       # ==== Examples
58:       #
59:       #   signed_id = User.first.signed_id expires_in: 15.minutes, purpose: :password_reset
60:       #
61:       #   User.find_signed signed_id # => nil, since the purpose does not match
62:       #
63:       #   travel 16.minutes
64:       #   User.find_signed signed_id, purpose: :password_reset # => nil, since the signed id has expired
65:       #
66:       #   travel_back
67:       #   User.find_signed signed_id, purpose: :password_reset # => User.first
68:       def find_signed(signed_id, purpose: nil, on_rotation: nil)
69:         raise UnknownPrimaryKey.new(self) if primary_key.nil?
70: 
71:         options = { on_rotation: on_rotation }.compact
72:         if id = signed_id_verifier.verified(signed_id, purpose: combine_signed_id_purposes(purpose), **options)
73:           find_by(primary_key => [id])
74:         end
75:       end
76: 
77:       # Works like find_signed, but will raise an ActiveSupport::MessageVerifier::InvalidSignature
78:       # exception if the +signed_id+ has either expired, has a purpose mismatch, is for another record,
79:       # or has been tampered with. It will also raise an ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound exception if
80:       # the valid signed id can't find a record.
81:       #
82:       # ==== Examples
83:       #
84:       #   User.find_signed! "bad data" # => ActiveSupport::MessageVerifier::InvalidSignature
85:       #
86:       #   signed_id = User.first.signed_id
87:       #   User.first.destroy
88:       #   User.find_signed! signed_id # => ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound
89:       def find_signed!(signed_id, purpose: nil, on_rotation: nil)
90:         options = { on_rotation: on_rotation }.compact
91:         if id = signed_id_verifier.verify(signed_id, purpose: combine_signed_id_purposes(purpose), **options)
92:           find(id)
93:         end
94:       end
95: 
96:       def signed_id_verifier
97:         if signed_id_verifier_secret
98:           @signed_id_verifier ||= begin
99:             secret = signed_id_verifier_secret
100:             secret = secret.call if secret.respond_to?(:call)
101: 
102:             if secret.nil?
103:               raise ArgumentError, "You must set ActiveRecord::Base.signed_id_verifier_secret to use signed IDs"
104:             end
105: 
106:             ActiveSupport::MessageVerifier.new secret, digest: "SHA256", serializer: JSON, url_safe: true
107:           end
108:         else
109:           return _signed_id_verifier if _signed_id_verifier
110: 
111:           if ActiveRecord.message_verifiers.nil?
112:             raise "You must set ActiveRecord.message_verifiers to use signed IDs"
113:           end
114: 
115:           ActiveRecord.message_verifiers["active_record/signed_id"]
116:         end
117:       end
118: 
119:       # Allows you to pass in a custom verifier used for the signed ids. This also allows you to use different
120:       # verifiers for different classes. This is also helpful if you need to rotate keys, as you can prepare

5: 
6: module Rails
7:   module Command
8:     class QueryCommand < Base # :nodoc:
9:       include EnvironmentArgument
10: 
11:       class_option :sql, type: :boolean,
12:         desc: "Treat input as raw SQL instead of an Active Record expression"
13: 
14:       class_option :database, aliases: "--db", type: :string,
15:         desc: "Database configuration to use (e.g. primary_replica)"
16: 
17:       class_option :page, type: :numeric, default: 1,
18:         desc: "Page number for paginated results"
19: 
20:       class_option :per, type: :numeric, default: 100,
21:         desc: "Results per page (max 10000)"
22: 
23:       desc "query [EXPRESSION]", "Run a read-only query against the database"
24:       def perform(expression = nil, *args)
25:         boot_application!
26:         Rails.application.load_runner
27: 
28:         ActiveSupport::Notifications.instrument("query.rails", expression: expression) do
29:           case expression
30:           when "schema"
31:             run_schema(args.first)
32:           when "models"
33:             run_models
34:           when "explain"
35:             run_explain(args.first)
36:           else
37:             run_query(expression)
38:           end
39:         end
40:       rescue StandardError, SyntaxError, NotImplementedError => e
41:         output_error(e.message)
42:         exit 1
43:       end
44: 
45:       private
46:         def run_schema(table = nil)
47:           with_readonly_connection do |connection|
48:             if table
49:               say format_table_detail(connection, table)
50:             else
51:               say format_table_list(connection)
52:             end
53:           end
54:         end
55: 
56:         def run_models
57:           Rails.application.eager_load!
58: 
59:           models = ActiveRecord::Base.descendants
60:             .reject(&:abstract_class?)
61:             .select { |model| model.table_name.present? }
62:             .sort_by(&:name)
63: 
64:           data = models.map do |model|
65:             {
66:               model: model.name,
67:               table_name: model.table_name,
68:               associations: format_associations(model)
69:             }
70:           end
71: 
72:           say JSON.generate(data)
73:         end
74: 
75:         def run_explain(expression = nil)
76:           expression = resolve_expression(expression)
77: 
78:           if options[:sql]
79:             with_readonly_connection do |connection|
80:               result = connection.select_all("EXPLAIN #{expression}")
81:               say format_result(columns: result.columns, rows: result.rows, sql: "EXPLAIN #{expression}")
82:             end
83:           else
84:             relation = with_readonly_connection { eval(expression, TOPLEVEL_BINDING, "(query)", 1) }

30:         if value.is_a?(Hash)

32:         elsif (idx = key.rindex("."))
33:           result << Arel.sql(key[0, idx], retryable: true)
34:         end

38:     # Define how a class is converted to Arel nodes when passed to +where+.
39:     # The handler can be any object that responds to +call+, and will be used
40:     # for any value that <tt>===</tt> the class given. For example:
41:     #
42:     #     MyCustomDateRange = Struct.new(:start, :end)
43:     #     handler = proc do |column, range|
44:     #       Arel::Nodes::Between.new(column,
45:     #         Arel::Nodes::And.new([range.start, range.end])
46:     #       )
47:     #     end
48:     #     ActiveRecord::PredicateBuilder.new("users").register_handler(MyCustomDateRange, handler)
49:     def register_handler(klass, handler)
50:       @handlers.unshift([klass, handler])
51:     end
52: 
53:     def [](attr_name, value, operator = nil)
54:       build(table.arel_table[attr_name], value, operator)
55:     end
56: 
57:     def build(attribute, value, operator = nil)
58:       value = value.id if value.respond_to?(:id)
59:       if operator ||= table.type(attribute.name).force_equality?(value) && :eq
60:         bind = build_bind_attribute(attribute.name, value)
61:         attribute.public_send(operator, bind)
62:       else
63:         handler_for(value).call(attribute, value)
64:       end
65:     end

68:       Relation::QueryAttribute.new(column_name, value, table.type(column_name))
69:     end

72:       table.associated_table(table_name, &block).arel_table[column_name]

89:             key = key.first
90:             value = value.flatten
91:           end
92: 
93:           if key.is_a?(Array)

95:               raise ArgumentError, "Expected corresponding value for #{key} to be an Array" unless ids_set.is_a?(Array)

98:             grouping_queries(queries)

101:               .predicate_builder.expand_from_hash(value.stringify_keys)
102:           elsif (associated_reflection = table.associated_with(key))

104:             # Post.where(author: author)
105:             #
106:             # For polymorphic relationships, find the foreign key and type:
107:             # PriceEstimate.where(estimate_of: treasure)
108: 
109:             if associated_reflection.polymorphic?
110:               value = [value] unless value.is_a?(Array)
111:               klass = PolymorphicArrayValue
112:             elsif associated_reflection.through_reflection?
113:               associated_table = table.associated_table(key)
114: 
115:               next associated_table.predicate_builder.expand_from_hash(
116:                 associated_table.primary_key => value

340:     #

344:       ExplainProxy.new(self, options)
345:     end

347:     # Converts relation objects to Array.
348:     def to_ary
349:       records.dup
350:     end

355:       @records
356:     end

358:     # Serializes the relation objects Array.
359:     def encode_with(coder)

361:     end
362: 
363:     # Returns size of the records.
364:     def size
365:       if loaded?
366:         records.length
367:       else
368:         count(:all)
369:       end
370:     end

372:     # Returns true if there are no records.
373:     def empty?
374:       return true if @none
375: 
376:       if loaded?
377:         records.empty?
378:       else
379:         !exists?
380:       end
381:     end
382: 
383:     # Returns true if there are no records.
384:     #
385:     # When a pattern argument is given, this method checks whether elements in
386:     # the Enumerable match the pattern via the case-equality operator (<tt>===</tt>).
387:     #
388:     #   posts.none?(Comment) # => true or false
389:     def none?(*args)
390:       return true if @none
391: 
392:       return super if args.present? || block_given?
393:       empty?
394:     end
395: 
396:     # Returns true if there are any records.
397:     #
398:     # When a pattern argument is given, this method checks whether elements in
399:     # the Enumerable match the pattern via the case-equality operator (<tt>===</tt>).
400:     #
401:     #    posts.any?(Post) # => true or false
402:     def any?(*args)
403:       return false if @none
404: 
405:       return super if args.present? || block_given?
406:       !empty?
407:     end
408: 
409:     # Returns true if there is exactly one record.

411:     # When a pattern argument is given, this method checks whether elements in
412:     # the Enumerable match the pattern via the case-equality operator (<tt>===</tt>).
413:     #
414:     #    posts.one?(Post) # => true or false
415:     def one?(*args)
416:       return false if @none
417: 
418:       return super if args.present? || block_given?
419:       return records.one? if loaded?
420:       limited_count == 1
421:     end
422: 
423:     # Returns true if there is more than one record.
424:     def many?
425:       return false if @none
426: 
427:       return super if block_given?
428:       return records.many? if loaded?
429:       limited_count > 1
430:     end

432:     # Returns a stable cache key that can be used to identify this query.
433:     # The cache key is built with a fingerprint of the SQL query.
434:     #
435:     #    Product.where("name like ?", "%Cosmic Encounter%").cache_key
436:     #    # => "products/query-1850ab3d302391b85b8693e941286659"
437:     #
438:     # If ActiveRecord::Base.collection_cache_versioning is turned off, as it was
439:     # in \Rails 6.0 and earlier, the cache key will also include a version.

924:     #   By default, automatic setting of timestamp columns is controlled by

933:     #
934:     # ==== Examples

942:     #   ], unique_by: :isbn)
943:     #
944:     #   Book.find_by(isbn: "1").title # => "Eloquent Ruby"

949:     # Updates the counters of the records in the current relation.
950:     #
951:     # ==== Parameters
952:     #
953:     # * +counter+ - A Hash containing the names of the fields to update as keys and the amount to update as values.
954:     # * <tt>:touch</tt> option - Touch the timestamp columns when updating.
955:     # * If attributes names are passed, they are updated along with update_at/on attributes.
956:     #
957:     # ==== Examples
958:     #
959:     #   # For Posts by a given author increment the comment_count by 1.
960:     #   Post.where(author_id: author.id).update_counters(comment_count: 1)
961:     def update_counters(counters)
962:       touch = counters.delete(:touch)
963: 
964:       updates = {}
965:       counters.each do |counter_name, value|
966:         attr = table[counter_name]
967:         updates[attr.name] = _increment_attribute(attr, value)
968:       end
969: 
970:       if touch
971:         names = touch if touch != true
972:         names = Array.wrap(names)
973:         options = names.extract_options!
974:         touch_updates = model.touch_attributes_with_time(*names, **options)
975:         updates.merge!(touch_updates) unless touch_updates.empty?
976:       end
977: 
978:       update_all updates
979:     end

981:     # Touches all records in the current relation, setting the +updated_at+/+updated_on+ attributes to the current time or the time specified.
982:     # It does not instantiate the involved models, and it does not trigger Active Record callbacks or validations.
983:     # This method can be passed attribute names and an optional time argument.
984:     # If attribute names are passed, they are updated along with +updated_at+/+updated_on+ attributes.
985:     # If no time argument is passed, the current time is used as default.
986:     #
987:     # ==== Examples
988:     #
989:     #   # Touch all records
990:     #   Person.all.touch_all
991:     #   # => "UPDATE \"people\" SET \"updated_at\" = '2018-01-04 22:55:23.132670'"
992:     #
993:     #   # Touch multiple records with a custom attribute
994:     #   Person.all.touch_all(:created_at)
995:     #   # => "UPDATE \"people\" SET \"updated_at\" = '2018-01-04 22:55:23.132670', \"created_at\" = '2018-01-04 22:55:23.132670'"
996:     #
997:     #   # Touch multiple records with a specified time
998:     #   Person.all.touch_all(time: Time.new(2020, 5, 16, 0, 0, 0))
999:     #   # => "UPDATE \"people\" SET \"updated_at\" = '2020-05-16 00:00:00'"
1000:     #
1001:     #   # Touch records with scope
1002:     #   Person.where(name: 'David').touch_all
1003:     #   # => "UPDATE \"people\" SET \"updated_at\" = '2018-01-04 22:55:23.132670' WHERE \"people\".\"name\" = 'David'"
1004:     def touch_all(*names, time: nil)
1005:       update_all model.touch_attributes_with_time(*names, time: time)
1006:     end
1007: 
1008:     # Destroys the records by instantiating each
1009:     # record and calling its {#destroy}[rdoc-ref:Persistence#destroy] method.
1010:     # Each object's callbacks are executed (including <tt>:dependent</tt> association options).
1011:     # Returns the collection of objects that were destroyed; each will be frozen, to
1012:     # reflect that no changes should be made (since they can't be persisted).
1013:     #
1014:     # Note: Instantiation, callback execution, and deletion of each
1015:     # record can be time consuming when you're removing many records at
1016:     # once. It generates at least one SQL +DELETE+ query per record (or
1017:     # possibly more, to enforce your callbacks). If you want to delete many
1018:     # rows quickly, without concern for their associations or callbacks, use
1019:     # #delete_all instead.

169:       # Detects if the value before type cast

171:       #
172:       # The fast cache version only works with a
173:       # string value directly from the database.
174:       #
175:       # We also must check if the timestamp format has been changed
176:       # or if the timezone is not set to UTC then
177:       # we cannot apply our transformations correctly.
178:       def can_use_fast_cache_version?(timestamp)
179:         timestamp.is_a?(String) &&
180:           cache_timestamp_format == :usec &&
181:           # FIXME: checking out a connection for this is wasteful
182:           # we should store/cache this information in the schema cache
183:           # or similar.
184:           self.class.with_connection(&:default_timezone) == :utc &&
185:           !updated_at_came_from_user?
186:       end
187: 
188:       # Converts a raw database string to `:usec`
189:       # format.
190:       #
191:       # Example:
192:       #
193:       #   timestamp = "2018-10-15 20:02:15.266505"
194:       #   raw_timestamp_to_cache_version(timestamp)
195:       #   # => "20181015200215266505"
196:       #
197:       # PostgreSQL truncates trailing zeros,
198:       # https://github.com/postgres/postgres/commit/3e1beda2cde3495f41290e1ece5d544525810214
199:       # to account for this we pad the output with zeros
200:       def raw_timestamp_to_cache_version(timestamp)
201:         key = timestamp.delete("- :.")
202:         if key.length < 20
203:           key.ljust(20, "0")
204:         else
205:           key
206:         end
207:       end
208:   end

todos.content: Step 1: Orient in the codebase - identify project structure and Relation location
Step 2: Map the Relation contract and shared modules
Step 3: Trace query build-up and execution
Step 4: Audit every dependent of the Relation contract
Step 5: Trace relation behavior reuse for associations
Step 6: Assess blast radius of contract change

content.text:
## Step 1 — Orient in the codebase

This is the Ruby on Rails monorepo. Each framework lives at the root; the ORM is `activerecord/`. The lazy query object is `ActiveRecord::Relation`, defined at:

- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation.rb:5`

The shared query-building pieces live beside it:

- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/query_methods.rb`
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/finder_methods.rb`
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/calculations.rb`
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/spawn_methods.rb`
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/merger.rb`
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/batches.rb`
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/delegation.rb`
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/predicate_builder.rb`

Relations are subclassed/reused by associations in:

- `activerecord/lib/active_record/associations/collection_proxy.rb`
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/association_relation.rb`
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/disable_joins_association_relation.rb`

Relations are born from the model layer in:

- `activerecord/lib/active_record/core.rb:442` (`Class#relation`)
- `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/delegation.rb:138` (`Relation.create`)

## Step 2 — Map the Relation contract and shared pieces

`ActiveRecord::Relation` is the concrete class at `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation.rb:5`. Its contract is:

- **Query state** is stored in `@values`, keyed by `VALUE_METHODS`
  - `MULTI_VALUE_METHODS` at `relation.rb:54`
  - `SINGLE_VALUE_METHODS` at `relation.rb:60`
  - `CLAUSE_METHODS` at `relation.rb:63`
  - `VALUE_METHODS` at `relation.rb:66`
- **Included modules** at `relation.rb:69-70`:
  - `FinderMethods` — `find`, `first`, `last`, `exists?`, etc.
  - `Calculations` — `count`, `sum`, `average`, `pluck`, etc.
  - `SpawnMethods` — `spawn`, `merge`, `except`, `only`
  - `QueryMethods` — `where`, `select`, `order`, `joins`, `includes`, etc.
  - `Batches` — `find_each`, `find_in_batches`
  - `Explain` — `explain`
  - `Delegation` — dynamic delegation to the model
  - `SignedId::RelationMethods` — `find_signed`
  - `TokenFor::RelationMethods` — `find_by_token_for`
- **Core accessors**: `table`, `model`, `loaded`, `predicate_builder`, `values`, `klass` alias at `relation.rb:72-74`
- **Spawning/merging contract**: `spawn` in `spawn_methods.rb:9`, `merge!` at `spawn_methods.rb:43`
- **Execution contract**: `records`/`load`/`exec_queries`/`exec_main_query` at `relation.rb:353`, `1212`, `1436`, `1460`
- **Materialization**: `to_a`/`to_ary` at `relation.rb:348`

What a teammate must know before changing it: the entire interface is built on `@values`, `spawn` returning a shallow-cloned relation, and `merge!` mutating values via `Relation::Merger`. Changing any of those three breaks every query method, association, scope, and merge path.

## Step 3 — Trace how a query is built up and executed

1. **Entry**: `User.all` → `Scoping::Named::ClassMethods#all` at `scoping/named.rb:22`
2. **Create base relation**: `Class#relation` at `core.rb:442` → `Relation.create(self)` → `Delegation::ClassMethods#create` at `delegation.rb:139` → `relation_class_for(model)` returns a per-model subclass
3. **Chain**: `User.where(...).limit(...)` → each public method calls `spawn` then the bang variant, e.g. `where` at `query_methods.rb:1113` → `spawn.where!`
4. **Spawn**: `SpawnMethods#spawn` at `spawn_methods.rb:9` returns `clone` (or `model.all` if already in scope)
5. **Merge**: `merge(other)` at `spawn_methods.rb:33` → `spawn.merge!(other)` → `Relation::Merger` at `merger.rb:43` copies `other.values` and applies each value bucket
6. **Execution**: `to_a` → `records` → `load` → `exec_queries` at `relation.rb:1436` → `exec_main_query` at `relation.rb:1460`
7. **SQL generation**: `exec_main_query` calls `arel` at `query_methods.rb:1673`, which visits `@values` and produces an Arel AST
8. **DB execution**: `model._query_by_sql(c, arel, async: async)` at `core.rb` / `querying.rb:67` → `connection.select_all`
9. **Instantiation**: `model._load_from_sql(rows)` at `querying.rb:71`

## Step 5 — Relation behavior reuse for associations

Associations reuse the relation contract in three subclasses:

1. **`Associations::CollectionProxy < Relation`** (`associations/collection_proxy.rb:31`)
   - Created by `CollectionAssociation#reader` at `associations/collection_association.rb:41`
   - Constructor stores `@association` and applies extensions
   - Overrides `find`, `load_target`, `records`, `size`, `empty?`, `delete_all`, `destroy_all`
   - `scope` at `collection_proxy.rb:953` exposes underlying association scope

2. **`AssociationRelation < Relation`** (`association_relation.rb:4`)
   - Used by singular associations
   - Created at `associations/association.rb:315`
   - Overrides `_new`, `_create`, `_create!`, `exec_queries` to wire inverse association and strict loading

3. **`DisableJoinsAssociationRelation < Relation`** (`disable_joins_association_relation.rb:4`)
   - Created by `DisableJoinsAssociationScope` at `associations/disable_joins_association_scope.rb:50`
   - Overrides `limit`, `first`, `load` to post-filter by id order

Association query construction flow:

- `Association#scope` at `associations/association.rb:107-117` returns the live relation
- It merges `target_scope` + `association_scope` + current/global scopes
- `AssociationScope#scope` at `associations/association_scope.rb:21-32` starts from `klass.unscoped`, applies joins/constraints, and returns a relation
- `CollectionAssociation#scope` at `associations/collection_association.rb:302-307` further applies `none!` for null scopes and `default_order!`
- `JoinDependency` reads `scope.arel` and scope value readers at `associations/join_dependency/join_association.rb:56`, `82`, `88`

## Step 6 — Blast radius of a Relation contract change

Ranked by risk:

**Critical — will break immediately if spawn/merge/values contract changes:**

1. `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/query_methods.rb` — all `VALUE_METHODS` readers/writers and every chainable method depend on `@values` shape
2. `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/spawn_methods.rb:9` — `spawn` is the only cloning primitive
3. `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/merger.rb:43-189` — reads `other.values` and mutates the relation
4. `activerecord/lib/active_record/associations/association.rb:107-117` and `collection_association.rb:302-307` — association scopes are built by merging relations
5. `activerecord/lib/active_record/associations/association_scope.rb:21-32`, `137-155` — builds association relations and directly mutates value buckets

**High — depend on `is_a?(Relation)` / `===` or on specific relation internals:**

6. `activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_handling.rb:419` — role/shard wrapper calls `.load` only on relations
7. `activerecord/lib/active_record/sanitization.rb:214` — bind-variable substitution for `Relation`
8. `activerecord/lib/active_record/encryption/extended_deterministic_queries.rb:28`, `44` — prepends `RelationQueries` and strips relation wrapper
9. `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/query_methods.rb:1206`, `1238`, `1654`, `1762`, `1782`, `1860`, `2008` — `#and`, `#or`, `from`, subquery value handling
10. `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/predicate_builder/relation_handler.rb:6-21` — subquery relation handling
11. `activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/abstract/database_statements.rb:803` — `arel_from_relation`
12. `activerecord/lib/active_record/scoping/default.rb:132` — default_scope validation

**Medium — subclasses and delegation:**

13. `activerecord/lib/active_record/associations/collection_proxy.rb` — subclass overrides `exec_queries`, `records`, `find`, etc.
14. `activerecord/lib/active_record/association_relation.rb` — subclass overrides `_new`, `_create`, `exec_queries`
15. `activerecord/lib/active_record/disable_joins_association_relation.rb` — subclass overrides `load`
16. `activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/delegation.rb` — per-model subclass cache, `uncacheable_methods`, `method_missing` delegation

**Cross-gem / CLI:**

17. `railties/lib/rails/commands/query/query_command.rb:174-183` — assumes `ActiveRecord::Relation` responds to `offset`, `limit`, `to_sql`, `model`
18. `activestorage/app/models/active_storage/blob.rb:150` — `scope_for_strict_loading` inspects `includes_values`
19. `activestorage/lib/active_storage/attached/model.rb` — `with_attached_*` scopes
20. `actiontext/lib/action_text/attribute.rb` — rich-text scopes

**Core extension / Enumerable collision risk:**

21. `activesupport/lib/active_support/core_ext/enumerable.rb` — `minimum`, `maximum`, `pluck`, `pick`, `sum` mirror relation methods; changing relation method arity/semantics can diverge behavior
